The structural principle is sound regardless: in secondary-nucleation-dominated aggregation, the number of fibril surfaces (catalytic sites) grows over time, so an effective nucleation rate increasing smoothly with normalized time is a reasonable physical proxy for the lag-to-burst transition. Since x0 is normalized to [0,1], `(1 + c4*x0)` stays bounded and well-conditioned.

I'll implement the breakthrough idea exactly: attach a bounded linear time-ramp `(1 + c4*time)` to the primary nucleation onset term only, keeping everything polynomial. This adds one degree of freedom targeting lag-phase placement without distorting plateau/elongation.
